text: China Spring Festival Gala just changed everything we thought we knew about robots. Unitry G1 flipped through the air doing real martial arts. Noixs made an actress out of silicone and code. Magic Labs robot pulled off a breakdancing move that gymnasts train years for. And Galbot quietly walked into your home and started doing chores. Hello guys, Alfie here. Welcome to AI Nexus. Hello guys, Alfie here. Welcome to AI Nexus. But let's begin with the performance that truly shocked the audience. Unitry. [music] When the G1 humanoid robot stepped onto the stage, they weren't there for a simple demo. They delivered what Unitary described as the world's first fully autonomous humanoid robot war martial arts show. Instead of a single robot performing alone, multiple G1 units moved together in perfect coordination, executing fast kicks, [music] sharp spins, and tightly synchronized formations. What made this performance stand out was that no human was controlling them with a remote. The robots relied entirely on advanced motion planning, balance systems, and AI coordination software to perform in real time. Their movements looked smooth, stable, [music] and surprisingly natural, showing a major leap forward compared to earlier robot stage performances. This level of precision was the result of months of preparation. Unit's team began rehearsals in November 2025, long before the spring festival gala. Behind the-scenes footage shows dozens of G1 robots practicing synchronized formations inside a hall marked with a floor grid, [music] which helped align each unit to fixed positions. Instead of controlling robots individually, Unitry used a centralized scheduling system that calculated timing, movement paths, and spacing. Each robot then executed those instructions using its motors, sensors, [music] and balance control algorithms with consistent accuracy. As the routine continued, the robots transitioned into the drunken fist segment, one of the most technically demanding parts of the performance. Drunken Fist is designed to look loose and offbalance, but it actually requires extremely precise coordination and timing. The G1 robots recreated the style by combining swaying footwork with sudden, fast strikes while maintaining stable posture. They handled wooden staffs and nunchucks with controlled grip strength, matching each swing to their body movement without losing balance. During the sequence, one robot completed a 7 and 1 half rotation aerial spin before landing steadily. Another dropped to the ground mid-rine and immediately rotated back to standing in a smooth recovery motion. These actions demonstrated how advanced their real-time balance control and motion correction systems have become. That 7 and 1/2 aerial spin from Unitry G1. Impressive or slightly terrifying? Drop your reaction in the comments. Behind the televised performance, another behind-the-scenes clip revealed something equally important. Unitry founder Wong Shing calmly walked through rows of active G1 robots as they executed synchronized movements around him. The robots were not reacting randomly or making independent decisions. They were following precisely engineered motion paths with strict timing control. Each unit relied on electric actuators, inertial sensors, and real-time balance algorithms to

Shing calmly walked through rows of active G1 robots as they executed synchronized movements around him. The robots were not reacting randomly or making independent decisions. They were following precisely engineered motion paths with strict timing control. Each unit relied on electric actuators, inertial sensors, and real-time balance algorithms to drifting off position. In another rehearsal moment near a padded wall, two G1 robots performed back flips while the founders stood between them. Back flips are extremely unstable for humanoid machines because they require exact torque output, controlled mid-air rotation, and precise landing force absorption. The fact that a human could safely stand so close showed Unit's confidence in their motion boundaries, collision planning, and repeatable multi-root coordination. We don't just show you robots flipping on stage. We explain what it actually means for the future. Subscribe so you don't miss what's coming next. After the G1 segment concluded, Unitry introduced the H2, their taller and more advanced full-sized humanoid for the sword dance performance. Due to its larger structure and extended reach, the H2 maintained careful distance from the young human performers on stage to ensure safety. The robot executed a controlled sword routine that highlighted its balance, joint precision, and coordinated upper body movement. In the closing moment, the H2 appeared dressed as the Monkey King while standing at top Unit's B2W quadriped robot, which carried it steadily across the stage in a symbolic and visually striking finale. By the end of the performance, Unitri had demonstrated several major technical milestones, including continuous freestyle parkour, a near 10-ft aerial flip, and a two-step wallass assisted backflip. These achievements showed that humanoid robots are no longer limited to control lab tests, but are advancing into realworld physical capability. But Unitatry wasn't the only story being told that night. Because while the G1 was out there proving that robots can move like athletes, another performance was happening on the same stage and it was asking a completely different question. Not can robots move like humans, but can robots feel like humans? And that question was answered in the most unexpected, most talked about moment of the entire gala. No, Robotics proved this with the grandma's favorite sketch. Their robots don't just move well. They're built to look and feel disturbingly human. Here's the setup. A grandson comes home after being away for a whole year. His gift to grandma, a power bank. [music] That's it. And grandma is not impressed because while he was gone, she replaced him with robots. Fully completely replaced him. These robots, including the Boommy, one of Noitics's flagship humanoid models, [music] known for its fluid movement and surprisingly natural physical presence, are doing everything the grandson should have been doing. Serving tea, performing back flips on a tiny 12 square meter stage, doing magic tricks, giving grandma cute little gestures just to make her feel loved. The grandson is just standing there watching machines do a better job at being family than he

Would that scare you or motivate you? Share your thoughts below. And then comes the twist that nobody saw coming. The grandma the audience had been watching the entire time. She was a robot, too. No built a 1:1 bionic replica of veteran actress Kaiing, the woman actually playing grandma, reportedly in as little as 30 to 90 days. The face had 32 degrees of freedom just for micro expressions, silicone skin that moved like real tissue, and an AI system syncing its movements to Kaiming's actual voice in real time. One of the most realistic humanoid androids ever shown on live television. The real Kaiming then walks out from backstage and suddenly this comedy sketch becomes something much heavier. Can a robot truly replace real human warmth? Can emotional connection be engineered? 30 years ago, Kai Ming performed a famous sketch where she played a human pretending to be a robot. In 2026, a robot was pretending to be her. That full circle moment landed differently than any backflip could because it didn't just show what robots can do. It made you feel something about it. If Noetics was the actor of the night, Magic Lab's Magicbot Z1 was absolutely the athlete. Right at the opening of the gala before people even had time to settle in, the Magicbot Z1 dropped a move that had never been done before by a humanoid robot. at this scale. It's called the Thomas 360. It's a break dancing power move where you hold your entire body up with just your arms and spin your legs in a big circle without them ever touching the ground. Gymnasts train for years to do this. This robot just did it. And they didn't stop there. During a big musical number called We Are Made in China, featuring some of China's biggest pop stars, six Magicbot Z1 units danced alongside the human performers. Then over at the Yeban sub venue, Magic Lab went full gymnastics mode. The Z1 performed single-hand jumps, balancing its entire body on one arm while jumping. It also pulled off 360° airborne kicks, landing cleanly even on the slick stage floor. But the moment that probably got the biggest reaction from the aerial cameras, hundreds of Magic Labs four-legged Magic Dog robots moving in perfect formation on the ground below, arranging themselves into the shape of a giant panda. arranging themselves into the shape of a giant panda. [music] It was part art installation, part technology flex, and completely unforgettable. Robots aren't just for stunts. They're ready to come into your home right now and actually be useful. Galbot is the official embodied AI partner of the Gala, and their robot is the G1, a wheeled humanoid with five dexterous hands. And instead of a livestage performance, they went with a short film called My Most Unforgettable Tonight, showing the G1 doing real household tasks. We're talking folding laundry, which sounds simple, but soft, floppy fabric is actually one of the hardest things for robots to handle. The G1 did it neatly. It also did kitchen prep, skewering sausages for a grill and cracking walnuts without crushing them. It picked up broken glass shards safely. It grabbed [music] specific items off high shelves. And in one moment that really showed off its language [music] skills, the G1 performed a Chinese tongue twister, syncing its speech with natural timing and social cues. What makes Galbat especially interesting is that this isn't just a demo. The G1 is already operating real convenience store kiosks in cities like Beijing, Shanghai, Shenzhen, Hjo, and Yibben. Regular people can actually walk into one of these stores and be served by this robot today. So, while other robots were getting applause for acrobatics, Galbat was quietly making the strongest argument of the night. That humanoid robots aren't just coming. For a lot of people in China, they're already here. This week in robotics, things got weird. Aubot just unleashed a kung fu humanoid that moves like a trained fighter. Another robot shape-shifted into a horse. Yes, a mechanical horse. Tron 2 reshapes itself into a mechanical horse configuration. And Unitry G1 is now building more G1 robots powered by a new AI model. This week in humanoid robotics was absolutely packed and we're covering all of it right now. And in the video, there's a guy standing in front of it who actually flinches and steps back like he's about to get hit. That's how fast and aggressive the movement is. This isn't some slow motion highlight reel, either. It's all happening at full speed. Now, what's actually going on under the hood? Aubot actually sent their robots to train with real Shaolin monks, not a PR stunt. The engineers used motion capture to study how monks move through kung fu stances. Because kung fu isn't just punching and kicking, right? It's about weight transfer, balance, and flowing between positions. Those same principles helped the team fine-tune how the A3 handles complex physical movement. But the bigger story might be what Ajabot pulled off back in February. They hosted a full 60-minute live gala called Aabot night. And the entire show was run by robots, robot hosts, robot dancers, robot comedians, robot magicians for a full hour. These things were doing group choreography, sharp spins, formation changes, magic tricks with human performers, even reacting to each other on stage like actual entertainers. At one point, humans jumped in and danced alongside the robots, and the machines matched their timing perfectly without missing a beat. What really stood out was the variety. Their full-sized A2 robots ran the show and handled transitions. The smaller X2 units brought personality with expressive movements. Industrial G2 models showed off precise handling, and the D1 quadripeds dressed up in panda and lion dance costumes walked through the crowd interacting with the audience. All of these different platforms running together in one space for an hour straight with zero visible breakdowns. They even brought robots to the United Nations in New York for Chinese New Year. The A2 did live calligraphy and had voice conversations with guests. The X2 performed kung fu routines. The D1's roamed the venue in traditional costumes. Completely different vibe from the gala, but the robots handled it just as smoothly. While Ajabot is perfecting motion, another company is rethinking form itself. [music] Limx Dynamics is back with Tron 2, and this time it's galloping into 2026 with a full year of the horse theme. For those who don't know, Tron 2, it's shape-shifting robot from Limx Dynamics that everyone said looked like a gaming PC that grew legs and walked off someone's desk. Yeah, it's [music] back and this time it's got a whole vibe going on. And in the latest showcase, Limx demonstrated something symbolic but technically important. Three separate Tron 2 units were combined to form a single mechanical horse. Now, this wasn't just a creative marketing moment. It was a structural proof point. The mechanical horse wasn't a separate product. It was built entirely from Tron 2 modules. Same control stack, same core hardware, just reassembled into a larger coordinated configuration. a creative marketing moment. It was a structural proof point. The mechanical horse wasn't a separate product. It was built entirely from Tron 2 modules. Same control stack, same core hardware, just reassembled into a larger coordinated configuration. That's the real story. Now, here's the thing. This isn't a spec secret sensor upgrade. Instead, Limx is doubling down on what makes Tron 2 different in the first place. Modular shape-shifting. Most robots are born one way and stay that way. One body, one job. End of story. Terron 2 doesn't play that game. From the beginning, this platform was built around a triform architecture. The same core system can run as a dual arm manipulation unit, a wheeled leg hybrid, or a full biped walker. That's not a cosmetic swap. That's a full physical reconfiguration while keeping the same control brain intact. And we've already seen what that brain can handle. This is the robot that hung from gymnastic rings using only its arms, then casually lifted a full-grown adult woman off the ground. No legs, no extra support, just controlled pull-ups like it was doing warm-up reps at the gym. On top of that, Tron 2 runs ultra low latency teley operation around 100 milliseconds. That means near real-time response, no awkward delay, no wait, [music] why did it move now? Moments. But what this new video really emphasizes is ecosystem thinking. Tron 2 isn't trying to look like a humanoid assistant. It still looks like a PC tower that decided it wanted freedom. And honestly, that's part of the charm. It's a developer first machine, a vision language action platform where data collection, training, and deployment live in one pipeline. Limx even claims new users can get started in about 2 hours. And this is exactly why we track these companies closely. Because the real story isn't just the demo, it's the system behind it. We break down what actually matters in the humanoid race. Subscribe to the AI Nexus to stay updated with the humanoid world. Tron 2 is redefining what a robot can become. Unitry is redefining what a robot can do. For years, the Unite G1 was the robot you saw doing kung fu demos and smooth stage performances. Back flips, sidekicks, crowd-pleasing tricks. That was its whole identity. Now the G1 is doing something far more important. It's building robots. Unitry puts several G1 units inside their own manufacturing facility. Not a demo room, not a controlled lab with perfect lighting. A real production floor, part staged, tools in position, and assembly stations ready to go. The G1s are picking up components, placing them with precision, and actively helping assemble real robot hardware. The movements are steady, deliberate, and repeatable. No dramatic flare, just work. This is humanoid robots entering manufacturing for real. The system behind it is called Unifol LMX10. It merges vision, language, and action into one unified AI model. hardware. The movements are steady, deliberate, and repeatable. No dramatic flare, just work. This is humanoid robots entering manufacturing for real. The system behind it is called Unifol LMX10. Early stages involved humanguided teley operation. Operators walked the robot through each [music] step manually. The AI learned from those demonstrations. Now the robot can repeat and adapt to similar tasks independently. Sorting parts, handling materials, assisting in subasssembly lines. And here's the bigger shift. These humanoids are helping manufacture more humanoids. That feedback loop matters. Every task performed in the factory generates more training data. More data sharpens the model. A sharper model improves the next batch of robots coming off the line. It's an acceleration cycle. And once it starts, it compounds. Now, let's be honest, there's still scaling to prove. Unitry confirmed the factory footage was shown at double speed. And right now, all of this is happening inside Unit's own facility. A familiar controlled environment sustained daily throughput across unfamiliar factories with different tools and layouts is a completely different challenge. But this moment is symbolic. The G1 is no longer just performing. The G1 is producing. And when humanoid robots begin assembling other robots inside active factories, the conversation shifts from demonstration to deployment.

title: China’s AI Robot Army Just Crossed the Line… Unitree G1 STUN, Noetix Robot Grandma, MagicBot Z1
